examples.title: Basic RBAC User-Role Assignment
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how to assign a user to a role in RBAC
examples.code:
FUNCTION AssignUserToRole(userID, roleID)
BEGIN
  // Check if user exists
  IF NOT UserExists(userID) THEN
    RETURN ERROR "User not found"
  ENDIF
  
  // Check if role exists
  IF NOT RoleExists(roleID) THEN
    RETURN ERROR "Role not found"
  ENDIF
  
  // Check constraints (e.g., separation of duties)
  IF NOT CheckConstraints(userID, roleID) THEN
    RETURN ERROR "Assignment violates security constraints"
  ENDIF
  
  // Add user to role
  INSERT INTO UserRoleTable (userID, roleID, assignedDate)
  VALUES (userID, roleID, CURRENT_TIMESTAMP)
  
  RETURN SUCCESS "User assigned to role"
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the process of assigning a user to a role, including validation checks for user and role existence, and constraint checking for security policies like separation of duties.

examples.title: DRBAC Permission Check Implementation
examples.description: Python implementation of checking permissions in DRBAC
examples.code:
class User:
    def __init__(self, user_id, name):
        self.user_id = user_id
        self.name = name
        self.roles = []
        
class Role:
    def __init__(self, role_id, name, parent_role=None):
        self.role_id = role_id
        self.name = name
        self.permissions = []
        self.parent_role = parent_role
        
    def get_all_permissions(self):
        perms = set(self.permissions)
        if self.parent_role:
            perms.update(self.parent_role.get_all_permissions())
        return perms
        
def check_permission(user, resource, operation):
    # Collect all permissions from all user roles
    user_permissions = set()
    for role in user.roles:
        user_permissions.update(role.get_all_permissions())
    
    # Check if required permission exists
    required_permission = (resource, operation)
    return required_permission in user_permissions

# Example usage
admin_role = Role("admin", "Administrator")
admin_role.permissions = [("system", "read"), ("system", "write"), ("system", "delete")]

user = User("u1", "John")
user.roles = [admin_role]

print(check_permission(user, "system", "write"))  # True
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates how DRBAC permission checking works by collecting all permissions from a user's assigned roles, including inherited permissions from parent roles in the hierarchy.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of roles
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is total permissions

examples.title: Role-Based Access Control in C
examples.description: C implementation showing basic RBAC data structures and permission checking
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates the basic data structures and algorithm for role-based access control. It shows how permissions are associated with roles and how a user's permissions are determined by checking all their assigned roles.
examples.timeComplexity: O(u × r × p) where u=roles per user, r=total roles, p=permissions per role
examples.spaceComplexity: O(r + p) for role and permission storage

examples.title: DRBAC Implementation with Role Hierarchy
examples.description: Java implementation demonstrating role hierarchy and inheritance in DRBAC
examples.explanation: This Java code demonstrates role hierarchy in DRBAC where roles can inherit permissions from parent roles. The Admin role inherits from Manager which inherits from Employee, so an Admin gets all three permissions (read, write, delete).
examples.timeComplexity: O(h × p) where h is hierarchy depth and p is permissions per role
examples.spaceComplexity: O(p) for storing permissions per role
